C. It proxies Spark commands from the local client to the remote cluster
D. It mirrors the entire cluster environment on the developer's machine
column2:
Answer: C

C. Databricks Connect acts as a **proxy**: PySpark code on the local client is serialized and sent to the remote cluster, which executes the actual Spark operations; only action results stream back to the local console. A is wrong — synchronization is automatic. B is wrong — compute runs remotely, not locally. D is wrong — the client doesn't mirror the cluster, it talks to it via Spark Connect. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A data engineer needs to configure an external location and a storage credential so that a new bronze ingestion pipeline can land data in cloud storage governed by Unity Catalog. The engineer has CREATE TABLE permission on schemas but receives a permission error when attempting to create the external location.
Which role is required to unblock this setup, the principle of least privilege?

A. Grant CREATE EXTERNAL LOCATION on both the metastore and the referenced storage credential so the engineer can define the external location.
B. Grant ALL PRIVILEGES on the catalog so the engineer can create external locations under its schemas.
C. Promote the engineer to Workspace Admin so they can manage objects and jobs.
D. Assign the engineer as Metastore Admin because only Metastore Admins can create external locations.
column2:
Answer: A

A. Creating an external location requires `CREATE EXTERNAL LOCATION` on the **metastore** AND on the **referenced storage credential** — that's the least-privilege grant. B (ALL PRIVILEGES on a catalog) doesn't include the metastore-level CREATE EXTERNAL LOCATION. C (workspace admin) is broad and orthogonal — workspace admin is not the same as metastore privilege. D (Metastore Admin) works but violates least privilege. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
After a failed multi-task job run, a data engineer changes the cluster size and updates a notebook path for one task. The data engineer wants to re-run just the failed/skipped tasks with the updated settings and keep the run lineage intact.
Which strategy should the data engineer use to preserve the run history?

A. Use Jobs REST API repairRun to re-run failed tasks; the repair uses the current job and task settings and appears in the run history.
B. Increase the task retry count and click 'Retry' on the failed task to automatically apply new cluster settings within the same run.
C. Export the job as a Databricks Asset Bundle, redeploy it, and re-run only the modified tasks from the bundle.
D. Use 'Run now' to start a new run, so that the updated settings apply; the new run replaces the prior run history.
column2:
Answer: A

A. **`POST /jobs/runs/repair`** (or the "Repair run" button) re-runs only the failed/skipped tasks, applies the current job and task settings (so the updated cluster size and notebook path are picked up), and appends the repair as a continuation of the original run — preserving lineage. B (retry count + Retry click) re-runs within the same task config and may not apply new cluster settings cleanly. C (export/redeploy as DAB) creates a different deployment artifact, not a repair. D (Run now) creates a new run, losing the link to the prior run history. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
When using MERGE INTO in Delta Lake on Databricks, which clause allows you to atomically delete target rows that no longer match any source rows?

A. WHEN MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN UPDATE SET NULL
B. WHEN MATCHED AND THEN DELETE
C. WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE
D. WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN DELETE
column2:
Answer: C

C. `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE` is the MERGE clause that removes target rows for which there is no corresponding source row — i.e. atomic "delete what's gone from the source." A (`WHEN MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN UPDATE SET NULL`) isn't a valid MERGE clause. B is syntactically broken. D (`WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN DELETE`) means "source row has no target match" — but you can't delete a target row that doesn't exist. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A data engineer must support self-serve BI dashboards for hundreds of business users who run ad hoc, high-concurrency SQL queries throughout the day against governed Unity Catalog tables. The team needs a near-instant start, autoscaling without manual tuning, and the best performance for SQL while minimizing operational overhead.
Which Databricks compute should the data engineer use?

A. SQL Warehouse (Classic) with a fixed cluster size.
B. Jobs compute clusters triggered by dashboards on a schedule.
C. All-purpose classic cluster with manual autoscaling enabled.
D. SQL Warehouse (Serverless) with Photon enabled.
column2:
Answer: D

D. **SQL Warehouse Serverless + Photon** is the canonical match: near-instant startup, auto-scaling for high concurrency without manual tuning, and Photon for fastest SQL execution. A (Classic, fixed size) lacks autoscale and instant start. B (jobs triggered by dashboards) is awkward and not low-latency. C (all-purpose cluster) is for code workloads, not ad-hoc SQL. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A Lakeflow Jobs workflow fails at the last task because of an error in a notebook task. This workflow is scheduled to run daily. The data engineer fixes the notebook and wants to rerun the pipeline. This workflow is very compute-intensive and expensive to run.
Which action should the data engineer take in order to minimize downtime and compute cost?

A. Restart the cluster
B. Re-run the entire workflow
C. Switch to another cluster
D. Repair run
column2:
Answer: D

D. **Repair run** re-executes only the failed task(s) and their downstream dependents, reusing successful upstream task outputs — minimising compute cost and downtime. A (restart cluster) doesn't re-execute the failed task. B (re-run entire workflow) re-runs successful tasks too, wasting compute. C (switch cluster) doesn't fix the failed task. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A data engineer is investigating a Lakehouse ETL job on Databricks that has suddenly become four times slower.
They need to:
• Identify the specific Spark stages that regressed.
• Drill down to per-task metrics such as shuffle read/write size, spill, and GC time.
• Correlate these with the executor resource usage during that run.
Which Databricks-native capability is the starting point for this detailed job-level root-cause analysis?

A. Cluster Metrics (Metrics tab on the compute) to view historical Spark and hardware metrics, without using the Spark UI.
B. The Jobs run details page timeline and graph views, without opening the Spark UI, to analyze all stage and task-level Spark metrics.
C. Ganglia metrics for the cluster, which provide full visibility into Spark stages, tasks, and shuffle metrics for the job run.
D. Spark UI opened from the job run's compute, using the Jobs and Stages tabs to inspect task metrics.
column2:
Answer: D

D. The **Spark UI**, accessed from the run's compute, is the only tool that gives stage-level, task-level, and shuffle/spill/GC metrics needed to diagnose a 4× regression. A (Cluster Metrics) shows hardware-level CPU/memory only — no Spark stage detail. B (Jobs run details page) shows task ordering and durations but lacks per-task Spark internals. C (Ganglia) is hardware/cluster-level resource monitoring, not Spark stage internals. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A data engineer is troubleshooting a Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines (SDP) where several rows are dropped by expectations. The product owner requests details on which expectation failed most frequently during the last run and the corresponding affected table. The engineer wants to use Databricks' built-in observability data to answer this without adding new logging code.
How should the data engineer troubleshoot SDP to verify the expectation failure?

A. Export notebook run results to HTML and search for the expectations summary in the rendered output to compile the metrics.
B. Query the Lakeflow Spark Declarative Pipelines event log to retrieve expectation metrics and failure details for the pipeline's most recent update.
C. Use the Spark UI's SQL tab to identify failed rows and expectation names from physical query plans.
D. Increase the cluster's log level to DEBUG and parse the driver's Log4j logs for expectation metrics for the last run.
column2:
Answer: B

B. The **DLT / Spark Declarative Pipelines event log** records per-update `flow_progress` events with `data_quality.expectations` metrics — counts of passed/dropped rows per expectation per dataset. Querying it gives the failure breakdown without writing custom logging. A (HTML export) doesn't include event-log data. C (Spark UI SQL tab) shows query plans, not expectation metrics. D (DEBUG Log4j) is heavy-handed and unstructured. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A data engineer is working with two tables. Each of these tables is displayed below in its entirety.
The data engineer runs the following query to join these tables together:
Which of the following will be returned by the above query?

A.
B.
C.
D.
E.
column2:
Answer: C

C. The query is a `LEFT JOIN` from `sales` (rows: a1, a3, a4) to `favorite_stores` (rows: a1→s1, a2→s1, a4→s2). LEFT JOIN preserves every row of `sales`; unmatched rows get `NULL` for the right-side columns. Result: a1 / 28.94 / s1, a3 / 874.12 / **NULL**, a4 / 8.99 / s2 — that's option C. Other options either drop the a3 row (treating it as INNER), include the a2 row (treating it as RIGHT JOIN), or duplicate rows. [Community vote] C: 23.

column1:
Which of the following is hosted completely in the control plane of the classic Databricks architecture?

A. Worker node
B. JDBC data source
C. Databricks web application
D. Databricks Filesystem
E. Driver node
column2:
Answer: C

C. The **Databricks web application** lives entirely in the Databricks-managed **control plane**. A (Worker node), D (DBFS), E (Driver node) are all in the customer's cloud account (data plane). B (JDBC data source) is external to Databricks entirely. [Community vote] C: 58, B: 2.

column1:
Which of the following describes the storage organization of a Delta table?

A. Delta tables are stored in a single file that contains data, history, metadata, and other attributes.
B. Delta tables store their data in a single file and all metadata in a collection of files in a separate location.
C. Delta tables are stored in a collection of files that contain data, history, metadata, and other attributes.
D. Delta tables are stored in a collection of files that contain only the data stored within the table.
E. Delta tables are stored in a single file that contains only the data stored within the table.
column2:
Answer: C

C. A Delta table is a **collection of files** in a single directory: Parquet files for the data, JSON/Checkpoint files in `_delta_log/` for the transaction history/metadata, and supporting files (stats, etc.). A and E (single file) are wrong — Delta is always multi-file. B (data in one file, metadata elsewhere) is wrong. D (only data files) ignores the transaction log. [Community vote] C: 31.

column1:
Which of the following code blocks will remove the rows where the value in column age is greater than 25 from the existing Delta table my_table and save the updated table?

A. SELECT * FROM my_table WHERE age > 25;
B. UPDATE my_table WHERE age > 25;
C. DELETE FROM my_table WHERE age > 25;
D. UPDATE my_table WHERE age <= 25;
E. DELETE FROM my_table WHERE age <= 25;
column2:
Answer: C

C. `DELETE FROM my_table WHERE age > 25` is the correct Delta Lake DML for removing matching rows in place. A (SELECT) returns rows but doesn't delete. B (`UPDATE ... WHERE` with no `SET`) is invalid syntax. D (UPDATE with no SET, wrong predicate) is invalid. E (DELETE with the inverted predicate) deletes the wrong rows. [Community vote] C: 23, A: 1.

column1:
Which of the following commands will return the number of null values in the member_id column?

A. SELECT count(member_id) FROM my_table;
B. SELECT count(member_id) - count_null(member_id) FROM my_table;
C. SELECT count_if(member_id IS NULL) FROM my_table;
D. SELECT null(member_id) FROM my_table;
column2:
Answer: C

C. `count_if(member_id IS NULL)` counts rows where the predicate is TRUE — i.e. the NULL count. A (`count(member_id)`) returns the **non-NULL** count (the opposite). B uses an invented function `count_null`. D (`null(member_id)`) is not a function. [Community vote] C: 8.

column1:
Which of the following data lakehouse features results in improved data quality over a traditional data lake?

A. A data lakehouse provides storage solutions for structured and unstructured data.
B. A data lakehouse supports ACID-compliant transactions.
C. A data lakehouse allows the use of SQL queries to examine data.
D. A data lakehouse stores data in open formats.
E. A data lakehouse enables machine learning and artificial Intelligence workloads.
column2:
Answer: B

B. **ACID transactions** prevent partial writes, dirty reads, and concurrent-write corruption — directly fixing the data-quality issues of plain data lakes. A (mixed structured/unstructured), C (SQL support), D (open formats), and E (ML support) are real lakehouse benefits but they're about flexibility/openness, not data quality. [Community vote] B: 69.

column1:
A data engineer wants to create a relational object by pulling data from two tables. The relational object does not need to be used by other data engineers in other sessions. In order to save on storage costs, the data engineer wants to avoid copying and storing physical data.
Which of the following relational objects should the data engineer create?

A. Spark SQL Table
B. View
C. Delta Table
D. Temporary view
column2:
Answer: D

D. A **temporary view** is session-scoped, references the source tables (no data materialised), and disappears at session end — perfect when no one else needs it and you want to avoid storage cost. A (Spark SQL table) and C (Delta table) physically store the result. B (View) is durable across sessions but the question explicitly limits scope to a single session. [Community vote] D: 12.

column1:
A data engineer has left the organization. The data team needs to transfer ownership of the data engineer’s Delta tables to a new data engineer. The new data engineer is the lead engineer on the data team.
Assuming the original data engineer no longer has access, which of the following individuals must be the one to transfer ownership of the Delta tables in Data Explorer?

A. Databricks account representative
B. This transfer is not possible
C. Workspace administrator
D. New lead data engineer
E. Original data engineer
column2:
Answer: C

C. When the original owner is gone, ownership transfer requires admin privileges — the **Workspace administrator** (or metastore admin in UC) is the right principal. A (Databricks rep) isn't part of the privilege model. B is wrong — transfer is possible. D (new lead) can't transfer to themselves without admin rights. E (original engineer) no longer has access per the scenario. [Community vote] C: 39.

column1:
A data analyst has created a Delta table sales that is used by the entire data analysis team. They want help from the data engineering team to implement a series of tests to ensure the data is clean. However, the data engineering team uses Python for its tests rather than SQL.
Which command could the data engineering team use to access sales in PySpark?

A. SELECT * FROM sales
B. spark.table("sales")
C. spark.sql("sales")
D. spark.delta.table("sales")
column2:
Answer: B

B. `spark.table("sales")` returns a DataFrame backed by the named table — the standard PySpark API for accessing a registered table. A is SQL, not PySpark. C (`spark.sql("sales")`) is wrong — `spark.sql()` takes a SQL *query*, e.g. `spark.sql("SELECT * FROM sales")`, not a table name. D (`spark.delta.table`) isn't a valid API. [Community vote] B: 1.

column1:
Which of the following commands will return the location of database customer360?

A. DESCRIBE LOCATION customer360;
B. DROP DATABASE customer360;
C. DESCRIBE DATABASE customer360;
D. ALTER DATABASE customer360 SET DBPROPERTIES ('location' = '/user'};
E. USE DATABASE customer360;
column2:
Answer: C

C. `DESCRIBE DATABASE customer360` (or `DESCRIBE SCHEMA customer360`) returns metadata including the **Location** of the database. A (`DESCRIBE LOCATION`) isn't a Spark SQL command. B (DROP) deletes it. D (ALTER) modifies properties. E (USE) just sets the current database. [Community vote] C: 31.

column1:
A data engineer wants to create a new table containing the names of customers that live in France.
They have written the following command:
A senior data engineer mentions that it is organization policy to include a table property indicating that the new table includes personally identifiable information (PII).
Which of the following lines of code fills in the above blank to successfully complete the task?

A. There is no way to indicate whether a table contains PII.
B. "COMMENT PII"
C. TBLPROPERTIES PII
D. COMMENT "Contains PII"
E. PII
column2:
Answer: D

D. To attach a human-readable comment to a table, use `COMMENT "..."` after the column list (or inside `CREATE TABLE`); for tagging PII a clear `COMMENT "Contains PII"` is the standard approach. A is wrong — there are multiple ways to tag PII (COMMENT, TBLPROPERTIES, Unity Catalog tags). B and E are missing the `COMMENT` keyword or use it incorrectly. C (`TBLPROPERTIES PII`) is malformed — TBLPROPERTIES requires `('key' = 'value')` pairs. [Community vote] D: 18.

column1:
Which of the following commands can be used to write data into a Delta table while avoiding the writing of duplicate records?

A. DROP
B. IGNORE
C. MERGE
D. APPEND
E. INSERT
column2:
Answer: C

C. **MERGE** (upsert) lets you match incoming rows against existing rows and `INSERT` only when not matched — so duplicates are never written. A (DROP) deletes the table. B (IGNORE) is not a standalone Delta command. D (APPEND) is a write mode that blindly appends, including duplicates. E (INSERT) inserts unconditionally, again allowing duplicates. [Community vote] C: 23, D: 1.

column1:
A data engineer runs a statement every day to copy the previous day’s sales into the table transactions. Each day’s sales are in their own file in the location "/transactions/raw".
Today, the data engineer runs the following command to complete this task:
After running the command today, the data engineer notices that the number of records in table transactions has not changed.
What explains why the statement might not have copied any new records into the table?

A. The format of the files to be copied were not included with the FORMAT_OPTIONS keyword.
B. The COPY INTO statement requires the table to be refreshed to view the copied rows.
C. The previous day’s file has already been copied into the table.
D. The PARQUET file format does not support COPY INTO.
column2:
Answer: C

C. `COPY INTO` is **idempotent**: it tracks which files have already been ingested and silently skips them on subsequent runs. If today's run included a file that was already loaded yesterday, COPY INTO would copy zero new rows. A (missing FORMAT_OPTIONS) would usually cause an error, not a silent no-op. B (refresh required) is wrong — COPY INTO commits in-place. D (Parquet unsupported) is false — Parquet is a supported COPY INTO format. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
In which of the following scenarios should a data engineer use the MERGE INTO command instead of the INSERT INTO command?

A. When the location of the data needs to be changed
B. When the target table is an external table
C. When the source is not a Delta table
D. When the target table cannot contain duplicate records
column2:
Answer: D

D. **MERGE INTO** is the right choice when the target table must **not contain duplicates** — it lets you check existence on a key and only insert when not matched. A (changing data location) is unrelated to MERGE vs INSERT. B (external table) — both commands work on external tables. C (non-Delta source) — both commands accept many source types; MERGE itself works on Delta targets. [Community vote] D: 3.

column1:
A data engineer needs to create a table in Databricks using data from their organization’s existing SQLite database.
They run the following command:
Which of the following lines of code fills in the above blank to successfully complete the task?

A. org.apache.spark.sql.jdbc
B. autoloader
C. org.apache.spark.sql.sqlite
D. sqlite
column2:
Answer: A

A. The JDBC source format string for Spark is `"org.apache.spark.sql.jdbc"` (or simply `"jdbc"`). B ("autoloader") is wrong format — Auto Loader is for cloud files, not JDBC. C (`org.apache.spark.sql.sqlite`) isn't a real Spark source. D ("sqlite") isn't a Spark format alias — sqlite is reached via the JDBC source with a sqlite driver. [Community vote] A: 5.

column1:
A data engineer needs access to a table new_table, but they do not have the correct permissions. They can ask the table owner for permission, but they do not know who the table owner is.
Which of the following approaches can be used to identify the owner of new_table?

A. Review the Permissions tab in the table's page in Data Explorer
B. There is no way to identify the owner of the table
C. Review the Owner field in the table's page in Data Explorer
D. Review the Owner field in the table's page in the cloud storage solution
column2:
Answer: C

C. In **Data Explorer / Catalog Explorer**, every table's overview page shows the **Owner** field — that's where you find who can grant you access. A (Permissions tab) lists who has what privileges, not the owner. B is wrong — the owner is always discoverable. D (cloud storage UI) shows storage-account ownership, not Databricks table ownership. [Community vote] C: 3.

column1:
A data engineer is attempting to drop a Spark SQL table my_table. The data engineer wants to delete all table metadata and data.
They run the following command:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS my_table -
While the object no longer appears when they run SHOW TABLES, the data files still exist.
Which of the following describes why the data files still exist and the metadata files were deleted?

A. The table’s data was larger than 10 GB
B. The table’s data was smaller than 10 GB
C. The table was external
D. The table did not have a location
E. The table was managed
column2:
Answer: C

C. For an **external** table, DROP TABLE only removes the metadata from the metastore — the underlying data files at the LOCATION are left intact (so other systems can keep using them). A and B (size) are irrelevant. D (no location) — external tables by definition have a LOCATION. E (managed) is the opposite: dropping a managed table deletes both metadata and data. [Community vote] C: 17.

column1:
A data engineer wants to create a data entity from a couple of tables. The data entity must be used by other data engineers in other sessions. It also must be saved to a physical location.
Which of the following data entities should the data engineer create?

A. Table
B. Function
C. View
D. Temporary view
column2:
Answer: A

A. A **Table** is a persistent, materialised data entity stored at a physical location and accessible across sessions to other users — exactly what the scenario requires. B (function) is not a data entity. C (view) is persistent and cross-session but holds no physical data — the scenario explicitly requires "saved to a physical location." D (temporary view) is session-scoped. [Community vote] A: 7, C: 2.

column1:
A data engineer is maintaining a data pipeline. Upon data ingestion, the data engineer notices that the source data is starting to have a lower level of quality. The data engineer would like to automate the process of monitoring the quality level.
Which of the following tools can the data engineer use to solve this problem?

A. Auto Loader
B. Unity Catalog
C. Delta Lake
D. Delta Live Tables
column2:
Answer: D

D. **Delta Live Tables** lets you declare `EXPECTATIONS` (e.g. `EXPECT (col IS NOT NULL)`) that automatically monitor incoming data quality, surface pass/fail counts in the event log and UI, and optionally drop or fail on violation. A (Auto Loader) ingests files. B (Unity Catalog) is governance. C (Delta Lake) is the storage layer. None of these provide automated quality monitoring out of the box. [Community vote] D: 3.

column1:
A data engineer and data analyst are working together on a data pipeline. The data engineer is working on the raw, bronze, and silver layers of the pipeline using Python, and the data analyst is working on the gold layer of the pipeline using SQL. The raw source of the pipeline is a streaming input. They now want to migrate their pipeline to use Delta Live Tables.
Which of the following changes will need to be made to the pipeline when migrating to Delta Live Tables?

A. The pipeline will need to be written entirely in Python
B. The pipeline will need to stop using the medallion-based multi-hop architecture
C. The pipeline will need to be written entirely in SQL
D. The pipeline will need to use a batch source in place of a streaming source
column2:
Answer: D

⚠️ The stated answer D appears incorrect. Delta Live Tables natively supports streaming sources via streaming live tables (Python: `@dlt.table` over `spark.readStream`, SQL: `CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE`), so the pipeline does NOT have to switch to a batch source. A is also wrong: DLT supports mixing Python and SQL within the same pipeline (one notebook per language, glued by the pipeline). B is wrong: the medallion (bronze/silver/gold) architecture is the recommended DLT pattern, not something to abandon. C is wrong for the same reason as A. The truthful answer is "no changes required," which is not among the options — flagging for review.

column1:
A data engineer has a Job that has a complex run schedule, and they want to transfer that schedule to other Jobs.
Rather than manually selecting each value in the scheduling form in Databricks, which of the following tools can the data engineer use to represent and submit the schedule programmatically?

A. pyspark.sql.types.DateType
B. datetime
C. pyspark.sql.types.TimestampType
D. Cron syntax
column2:
Answer: D

D. **Cron syntax** is the standard string-based, portable representation of a recurring schedule — Databricks Jobs accepts a `quartz_cron_expression` so you can copy/paste the same schedule between jobs (and version-control it). A, B, C are Python data types for representing single dates/times, not schedules. [Community vote] D: 4.

column1:
A data analyst has developed a query that runs against Delta table. They want help from the data engineering team to implement a series of tests to ensure the data returned by the query is clean. However, the data engineering team uses Python for its tests rather than SQL.
Which of the following operations could the data engineering team use to run the query and operate with the results in PySpark?

A. SELECT * FROM sales
B. spark.delta.table
C. spark.sql
D. spark.table
column2:
Answer: C

C. `spark.sql("SELECT ...")` runs an arbitrary SQL query and returns a DataFrame — perfect when the analyst already has a SQL query and the engineers want to test the results in Python. A is the SQL itself, not Python. B (`spark.delta.table`) isn't an API. D (`spark.table`) accesses a *table by name* but the scenario gives you a *query*, not a table reference. [Community vote] C: 4, D: 1.

column1:
A data organization leader is upset about the data analysis team’s reports being different from the data engineering team’s reports. The leader believes the siloed nature of their organization’s data engineering and data analysis architectures is to blame.
Which of the following describes how a data lakehouse could alleviate this issue?

A. Both teams would respond more quickly to ad-hoc requests
B. Both teams would use the same source of truth for their work
C. Both teams would reorganize to report to the same department
D. Both teams would be able to collaborate on projects in real-time
column2:
Answer: B

B. The defining benefit of a **lakehouse** is one governed copy of the data serving both engineering and analytics — eliminating the warehouse/lake silo that causes mismatching numbers. A (ad-hoc speed), C (org reporting), D (collaboration) are nice but don't address the *root cause* (different teams reading different sources). [Community vote] B: 5.

column1:
Which Structured Streaming query is performing a hop from a Silver table to a Gold table?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: D

D. A Silver→Gold hop in Structured Streaming typically reads from a Silver source, applies aggregations (Gold is the business-aggregated layer), and writes back with `.writeStream` using `outputMode("complete")` (required for aggregations). Option D shows exactly that pattern: reading a `sales` table, aggregating with `groupBy("store").agg(sum("sales"))`, and `.writeStream...outputMode("complete").table(...)`. The other options either omit aggregations, use the wrong output mode, or write back to the same source. [Community vote] D: 9, B: 1.

column1:
A data engineer has configured a Structured Streaming job to read from a table, manipulate the data, and then perform a streaming write into a new table.
The cade block used by the data engineer is below:
If the data engineer only wants the query to execute a micro-batch to process data every 5 seconds, which of the following lines of code should the data engineer use to fill in the blank?

A. trigger("5 seconds")
B. trigger()
C. trigger(once="5 seconds")
D. trigger(processingTime="5 seconds")
E. trigger(continuous="5 seconds")
column2:
Answer: D

D. `trigger(processingTime="5 seconds")` is the Structured Streaming trigger that fires a micro-batch every 5 seconds. A (`trigger("5 seconds")`) is missing the keyword arg. B (`trigger()`) is invalid — trigger needs an arg. C (`trigger(once="5 seconds")`) — `once` is a boolean, not a duration (it runs the query exactly once then stops). E (`trigger(continuous="5 seconds")`) is the experimental continuous-processing mode, which is checkpoint-frequency-based and a different paradigm — not the standard micro-batch interval. [Community vote] D: 18.

column1:
Which of the following describes when to use the CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE (formerly CREATE INCREMENTAL LIVE TABLE) syntax over the CREATE LIVE TABLE syntax when creating Delta Live Tables (DLT) tables using SQL?

A. CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE should be used when the subsequent step in the DLT pipeline is static.
B. CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE should be used when data needs to be processed incrementally.
C. CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE is redundant for DLT and it does not need to be used.
D. CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE should be used when data needs to be processed through complicated aggregations.
E. CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE should be used when the previous step in the DLT pipeline is static.
column2:
Answer: B

B. `CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE` (now "STREAMING TABLE") is for **incremental** processing — each pipeline update processes only new records since the last checkpoint. `CREATE LIVE TABLE` (now "MATERIALIZED VIEW") fully recomputes every update. A and E about the adjacent step's nature are irrelevant. C is wrong — the choice matters. D (complicated aggregations) actually favours full-recompute MATERIALIZED VIEW, since aggregating a stream incrementally is tricky. [Community vote] B: 27.

column1:
A data engineer has joined an existing project and they see the following query in the project repository:
CREATE STREAMING LIVE TABLE loyal_customers AS
SELECT customer_id -
FROM STREAM(LIVE.customers)
WHERE loyalty_level = 'high';
Which of the following describes why the STREAM function is included in the query?

A. The STREAM function is not needed and will cause an error.
B. The data in the customers table has been updated since its last run.
C. The customers table is a streaming live table.
D. The customers table is a reference to a Structured Streaming query on a PySpark DataFrame.
column2:
Answer: C

C. The `STREAM(LIVE.customers)` wrapper tells DLT to read `customers` as a **stream** — which is required when `customers` is itself a STREAMING LIVE TABLE (a stream-only source). If you omitted `STREAM()`, you'd be doing a full batch read. A (not needed) is wrong. B ("updated since last run") describes streaming behaviour but doesn't explain the *requirement* for STREAM. D conflates DLT with PySpark Structured Streaming. [Community vote] C: 5.

column1:
A data engineer wants to schedule their Databricks SQL dashboard to refresh once per day, but they only want the associated SQL endpoint to be running when it is necessary.
Which of the following approaches can the data engineer use to minimize the total running time of the SQL endpoint used in the refresh schedule of their dashboard?

A. They can ensure the dashboard’s SQL endpoint matches each of the queries’ SQL endpoints.
B. They can set up the dashboard’s SQL endpoint to be serverless.
C. They can turn on the Auto Stop feature for the SQL endpoint.
D. They can reduce the cluster size of the SQL endpoint.
E. They can ensure the dashboard’s SQL endpoint is not one of the included query’s SQL endpoint.
column2:
Answer: C

C. **Auto Stop** terminates the SQL endpoint after a configurable idle period — perfect when a daily dashboard refresh is the only workload. A (matching endpoints) doesn't reduce runtime. B (serverless) speeds startup but the warehouse still runs while queries run. D (smaller cluster) makes queries slower, not the endpoint shorter-lived. E (separate endpoints) is not directly about runtime. [Community vote] C: 26.

column1:
A data engineer has a Job with multiple tasks that runs nightly. Each of the tasks runs slowly because the clusters take a long time to start.
Which of the following actions can the data engineer perform to improve the start up time for the clusters used for the Job?

A. They can use endpoints available in Databricks SQL
B. They can use jobs clusters instead of all-purpose clusters
C. They can configure the clusters to be single-node
D. They can use clusters that are from a cluster pool
E. They can configure the clusters to autoscale for larger data sizes
column2:
Answer: D

D. **Cluster pools** maintain idle, pre-warmed VMs; jobs that draw from the pool skip the cloud-VM-provisioning step (often 3-5 minutes). A (SQL endpoints) is for SQL queries. B (jobs vs all-purpose) both cold-start equally. C (single-node) just changes node count, not startup latency. E (autoscale) is a runtime concern, not startup. [Community vote] D: 25, B: 2, C: 1.

column1:
A new data engineering team team. has been assigned to an ELT project. The new data engineering team will need full privileges on the database customers to fully manage the project.
Which of the following commands can be used to grant full permissions on the database to the new data engineering team?

A. GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE customers TO team;
B. GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE team TO customers;
C. GRANT SELECT PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE customers TO teams;
D. GRANT SELECT CREATE MODIFY USAGE PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE customers TO team;
E. GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE customers TO team;
column2:
Answer: E

E. `GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE customers TO team` is the correct SQL to give the team full rights at the database/schema level. A (USAGE only) lets them see but not modify. B has principal/object swapped. C is malformed (`SELECT PRIVILEGES` isn't valid syntax; also wrong recipient). D invents a multi-keyword syntax that isn't valid (you'd need separate GRANT statements, and CREATE is correct but the chain is malformed). [Community vote] E: 14, D: 2.

column1:
A new data engineering team has been assigned to work on a project. The team will need access to database customers in order to see what tables already exist. The team has its own group team.
Which of the following commands can be used to grant the necessary permission on the entire database to the new team?

A. GRANT VIEW ON CATALOG customers TO team;
B. GRANT CREATE ON DATABASE customers TO team;
C. GRANT USAGE ON CATALOG team TO customers;
D. GRANT CREATE ON DATABASE team TO customers;
E. GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE customers TO team;
column2:
Answer: E

E. `GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE customers TO team` gives the team the right to **see and reference** objects in that database (a prerequisite for SHOW TABLES and SELECT). A uses `VIEW` (not a privilege) and wrong scope (`CATALOG`). B (CREATE) lets them create tables, not list existing ones. C and D swap principal and object. [Community vote] E: 31, A: 7, B: 2.

column1:
A data engineer is running code in a Databricks Repo that is cloned from a central Git repository. A colleague of the data engineer informs them that changes have been made and synced to the central Git repository. The data engineer now needs to sync their Databricks Repo to get the changes from the central Git repository.
Which Git operation does the data engineer need to run to accomplish this task?

A. Clone
B. Pull
C. Merge
D. Push
column2:
Answer: B

B. **Pull** fetches and integrates changes from a remote into the local clone — exactly what's needed to sync up after a colleague has pushed changes. A (Clone) is for the initial copy. C (Merge) is part of what pull does internally, but the user-facing operation is pull. D (Push) sends local commits to the remote (the opposite direction). [Community vote] B: 2.

column1:
Which of the following describes a scenario in which a data engineer will want to use a single-node cluster?

A. When they are working interactively with a small amount of data
B. When they are running automated reports to be refreshed as quickly as possible
C. When they are working with SQL within Databricks SQL
D. When they are concerned about the ability to automatically scale with larger data
E. When they are manually running reports with a large amount of data
column2:
Answer: A

A. A **single-node cluster** has a driver only (no workers), which is ideal for **interactive work on small datasets** (light Python/ML/single-machine libraries) — cheaper, faster to start, no inter-node shuffle overhead. B (fast automated reports) wants cluster pools, not single-node. C (Databricks SQL) uses SQL Warehouses, not clusters. D (autoscale to large data) is exactly what single-node can't do. E (large manual reports) needs a multi-node cluster. [Community vote] A: 8.

column1:
Which of the following describes the relationship between Bronze tables and raw data?

A. Bronze tables contain less data than raw data files.
B. Bronze tables contain more truthful data than raw data.
C. Bronze tables contain raw data with a schema applied.
D. Bronze tables contain a less refined view of data than raw data.
column2:
Answer: C

C. **Bronze** holds raw data with a schema applied (so it's queryable as a Delta table) but **no other transformations**. A is generally false — Bronze typically has the same row count as raw. B ("more truthful") is subjective and not the structural difference. D inverts the relationship (Bronze is *more* refined than raw, having a schema). [Community vote] C: 2.

column1:
A data engineer has realized that they made a mistake when making a daily update to a table. They need to use Delta time travel to restore the table to a version that is 3 days old. However, when the data engineer attempts to time travel to the older version, they are unable to restore the data because the data files have been deleted.
Which of the following explains why the data files are no longer present?

A. The VACUUM command was run on the table
B. The TIME TRAVEL command was run on the table
C. The DELETE HISTORY command was run on the table
D. The OPTIMIZE command was nun on the table
column2:
Answer: A

A. **VACUUM** permanently deletes data files that are no longer referenced by recent table versions (default retention 7 days). If VACUUM ran with a short retention, files needed for older versions get removed and time travel to them fails. B (`TIME TRAVEL`) isn't a separate command. C (`DELETE HISTORY`) isn't a Delta command. D (OPTIMIZE) compacts files but does **not** delete data files needed for older versions — only VACUUM does that. [Community vote] A: 2.

column1:
A data engineer needs to apply custom logic to string column city in table stores for a specific use case. In order to apply this custom logic at scale, the data engineer wants to create a SQL user-defined function (UDF).
Which of the following code blocks creates this SQL UDF?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: A

A. The correct SQL UDF syntax is `CREATE FUNCTION <name>(<arg> <type>) RETURNS <type> RETURN <expression>`. Option A shows `CREATE FUNCTION combine_nyc(city STRING) RETURNS STRING RETURN CASE WHEN city = "brooklyn" THEN "new york" ELSE city END;` — valid SQL UDF. The other options have errors like missing RETURN keyword, wrong RETURNS position, or use Python-style syntax (DEF, def). [Community vote] A: 4.

column1:
An engineering manager uses a Databricks SQL query to monitor ingestion latency for each data source. The manager checks the results of the query every day, but they are manually rerunning the query each day and waiting for the results.
Which of the following approaches can the manager use to ensure the results of the query are updated each day?

A. They can schedule the query to refresh every 1 day from the SQL endpoint's page in Databricks SQL.
B. They can schedule the query to refresh every 12 hours from the SQL endpoint's page in Databricks SQL.
C. They can schedule the query to refresh every 1 day from the query's page in Databricks SQL.
D. They can schedule the query to run every 12 hours from the Jobs UI.
column2:
Answer: C

C. Databricks SQL queries have a per-query scheduler (Schedule button on the query's page); the manager sets it to refresh every 1 day. A and B are wrong because the schedule belongs to the *query*, not to the *SQL endpoint*. D would use Jobs UI, which is for notebooks/scripts, not for ad-hoc SQL query refreshes (and the wrong interval). [Community vote] C: 2.

column1:
Which of the following SQL keywords can be used to convert a table from a long format to a wide format?

A. TRANSFORM
B. PIVOT
C. SUM
D. CONVERT
E. WHERE
column2:
Answer: B

B. **PIVOT** rotates rows into columns (long → wide). A (TRANSFORM) applies a function to elements; not a reshape. C (SUM) is an aggregate. D (CONVERT) is type casting in other dialects, not a reshape. E (WHERE) is a filter. [Community vote] B: 8.

column1:
The Delta transaction log for the ‘students’ tables is shown using the ‘DESCRIBE HISTORY students’ command. A Data Engineer needs to query the table as it existed before the UPDATE operation listed in the log.
Which command should the Data Engineer use to achieve this? (Choose two.)

A. SELECT * FROM students@v4
B. SELECT * FROM students TIMESTAMP AS OF ‘2024-04-22T 14:32:47.000+00:00’
C. SELECT * FROM students FROM HISTORY VERSION AS OF 3
D. SELECT * FROM students VERSION AS OF 5
E. SELECT * FROM students TIMESTAMP AS OF ‘2024-04-22T 14:32:58.000+00:00’
column2:
Answer: AB

A and B. To query the table **before** the UPDATE, you need the version (or timestamp) that came *immediately before* it. A (`@v4` shorthand) and B (the earlier timestamp `14:32:47`) both point to that pre-UPDATE version. C uses invalid syntax (`FROM HISTORY VERSION AS OF`). D (`VERSION AS OF 5`) points to the UPDATE version itself, not before it. E (timestamp `14:32:58`) is after the UPDATE.

[Community vote] AB: 8 (matches stated), BD: 3. The minority is reading version 5 as the pre-UPDATE state — this depends on whether version 5 is the UPDATE itself or the state after some prior operation; here AB is the consistent reading.

column1:
A data engineer needs to apply custom logic to identify employees with more than 5 years of experience in array column employees in table stores. The custom logic should create a new column exp_employees that is an array of all of the employees with more than 5 years of experience for each row. In order to apply this custom logic at scale, the data engineer wants to use the FILTER higher-order function.
Which code block successfully completes this task?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: A

A. The correct pattern for the higher-order `FILTER` function in Spark SQL is `FILTER(employees, e -> e.years_experience > 5)` — applied via a SELECT with `AS exp_employees`. Option A shows that pattern. Other options use wrong syntax (e.g. invalid lambda form, wrong function name, or filter the rows of the table rather than the array elements). [Community vote] A: 7, B: 1.

column1:
A data engineer that is new to using Python needs to create a Python function to add two integers together and return the sum?
Which of the following code blocks can the data engineer use to complete this task?

A.
B.
C.
D.
E.
column2:
Answer: D

D. Python function definitions use `def name(args):` followed by an indented block ending in `return`. Option D shows the canonical pattern (`def add(a, b): return a + b`). Other options use wrong keyword order, miss the colon, miss the return, or use non-Python syntax. [Community vote] D: 11.

column1:
A data engineer has configured a Structured Streaming job to read from a table, manipulate the data, and then perform a streaming write into a new table.
The code block used by the data engineer is below:
Which line of code should the data engineer use to fill in the blank if the data engineer only wants the query to execute a micro-batch to process data every 5 seconds?

A. trigger("5 seconds")
B. trigger(continuous="5 seconds")
C. trigger(once="5 seconds")
D. trigger(processingTime="5 seconds")
column2:
Answer: D

D. `trigger(processingTime="5 seconds")` schedules a micro-batch every 5 seconds — the documented way to set the micro-batch interval. A is invalid syntax: `trigger` has no positional string argument. B (`continuous=...`) selects the experimental continuous processing mode (no micro-batches), not what the question asks. C (`once=...`) is meant for one-shot processing and does not take a time interval.

column1:
A data engineer has developed a data pipeline to ingest data from a JSON source using Auto Loader, but the engineer has not provided any type inference or schema hints in their pipeline. Upon reviewing the data, the data engineer has noticed that all of the columns in the target table are of the string type despite some of the fields only including float or boolean values.
Why has Auto Loader inferred all of the columns to be of the string type?

A. Auto Loader cannot infer the schema of ingested data
B. JSON data is a text-based format
C. Auto Loader only works with string data
D. All of the fields had at least one null value
column2:
Answer: B

B. Auto Loader does infer schema for JSON (so A is false), but because JSON is a text-based format with no inherent type information — `123` and `"123"` look the same on the wire — Auto Loader's default is to read every column as STRING. To get typed columns you either set `cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes=true` or supply schema hints. A directly contradicts how Auto Loader actually works. C is false — Auto Loader works with many data types under the right configuration. D is false — nulls do not force string coercion.

column1:
Which of the following data workloads will utilize a Gold table as its source?

A. A job that enriches data by parsing its timestamps into a human-readable format
B. A job that aggregates uncleaned data to create standard summary statistics
C. A job that cleans data by removing malformatted records
D. A job that queries aggregated data designed to feed into a dashboard
E. A job that ingests raw data from a streaming source into the Lakehouse
column2:
Answer: D

D. **Gold** tables are the business-aggregated layer designed for direct consumption by dashboards and reports. A (parsing timestamps) is a Silver-layer cleanup. B (aggregating raw/unclean data) is wrong because Gold builds from clean Silver. C (cleaning) is Silver. E (raw streaming ingest) is Bronze. [Community vote] D: 3.

column1:
Which two components function in the DB platform architecture’s control plane? (Choose two.)

A. Virtual Machines
B. Compute Orchestration
C. Serverless Compute
D. Compute
E. Unity Catalog
column2:
Answer: BE

B and E. The **control plane** holds platform services: **Compute Orchestration** (cluster manager) and **Unity Catalog** (governance/metastore) both live there. A (VMs), C (Serverless Compute), D (Compute) are all compute resources that live in the data plane (customer cloud account) or Databricks' serverless compute plane — they're not control-plane services. [Community vote] BE: 3.

column1:
Which of the following describes the type of workloads that are always compatible with Auto Loader?

A. Streaming workloads
B. Machine learning workloads
C. Serverless workloads
D. Batch workloads
E. Dashboard workloads
column2:
Answer: A

A. Auto Loader is fundamentally a **Structured Streaming** source — streaming workloads are its canonical use. Note: Auto Loader can also be run batch-style via `trigger(availableNow=True)`, but D's wording ("workloads always compatible") points to the always-streaming-compatible mode. B (ML), C (serverless), E (dashboards) aren't workload types tied to Auto Loader. [Community vote] A: 7.

column1:
A data engineer is using the following code block as part of a batch ingestion pipeline to read from a composable table:
Which of the following changes needs to be made so this code block will work when the transactions table is a stream source?

A. Replace predict with a stream-friendly prediction function
B. Replace schema(schema) with option ("maxFilesPerTrigger", 1)
C. Replace "transactions" with the path to the location of the Delta table
D. Replace format("delta") with format("stream")
E. Replace spark.read with spark.readStream
column2:
Answer: E

E. To convert a batch read into a streaming read, replace **`spark.read`** with **`spark.readStream`** — Delta supports both, so the rest of the chain (`.format("delta").table(...)`) stays the same. A (predict function) is unrelated. B (maxFilesPerTrigger) is a streaming option but doesn't switch the read mode. C (path vs name) is unrelated to streaming vs batch. D (`format("stream")`) isn't a valid format. [Community vote] E: 9.

column1:
Which of the following queries is performing a streaming hop from raw data to a Bronze table?

A.
B.
C.
D.
E.
column2:
Answer: E

E. A streaming **raw → Bronze** hop reads raw files with `spark.readStream.format("cloudFiles")` (Auto Loader) or similar streaming source and writes to a Delta table with `.writeStream...table("bronze")`. Option E shows that canonical pattern. The other options either use batch reads/writes, target the wrong layer, or apply transformations (transformations belong in Silver, not the raw→Bronze hop). [Community vote] E: 10.

column1:
Which of the following statements regarding the relationship between Silver tables and Bronze tables is always true?

A. Silver tables contain a less refined, less clean view of data than Bronze data.
B. Silver tables contain aggregates while Bronze data is unaggregated.
C. Silver tables contain more data than Bronze tables.
D. Silver tables contain a more refined and cleaner view of data than Bronze tables.
E. Silver tables contain less data than Bronze tables.
column2:
Answer: D

D. The defining property of **Silver** is that it's a **more refined, cleaner view** of Bronze (deduplicated, schema-conformed, validated). A inverts the relationship. B is wrong — Silver is row-level, aggregates live in Gold. C — Silver typically has *less* (filtered/deduplicated) data than Bronze. E (less data) is often true but not always; it's a side-effect, not the defining property the question asks for. [Community vote] D: 4.

column1:
Which of the following approaches should be used to send the Databricks Job owner an email in the case that the Job fails?

A. Manually programming in an alert system in each cell of the Notebook
B. Setting up an Alert in the Job page
C. Setting up an Alert in the Notebook
D. There is no way to notify the Job owner in the case of Job failure
E. MLflow Model Registry Webhooks
column2:
Answer: B

B. Databricks Jobs have built-in **Alert/Notification settings** on the Job page: you specify recipients (job owner, group, email) for events like start, success, failure. A (manual cell alerts) is fragile and incomplete. C (notebook alerts) isn't a feature. D is wrong — Jobs absolutely support failure notifications. E (MLflow Webhooks) is for model registry events, not jobs. [Community vote] B: 5.

column1:
A data engineer has been using a Databricks SQL dashboard to monitor the cleanliness of the input data to a data analytics dashboard for a retail use case. The job has a Databricks SQL query that returns the number of store-level records where sales is equal to zero. The data engineer wants their entire team to be notified via a messaging webhook whenever this value is greater than 0.
Which of the following approaches can the data engineer use to notify their entire team via a messaging webhook whenever the number of stores with $0 in sales is greater than zero?

A. They can set up an Alert with a custom template.
B. They can set up an Alert with a new email alert destination.
C. They can set up an Alert with one-time notifications.
D. They can set up an Alert with a new webhook alert destination.
E. They can set up an Alert without notifications.
column2:
Answer: D

D. Databricks SQL Alerts support "notification destinations," including webhook destinations (Slack, MS Teams, generic HTTP) — exactly the integration the question describes. A (custom template) changes the message body, not the channel. B uses email only, not a webhook. C (one-time notification) would alert once then stop firing — wrong for ongoing monitoring. E disables notifications entirely.

column1:
A data engineer wants to schedule their Databricks SQL dashboard to refresh every hour, but they only want the associated SQL endpoint to be running when it is necessary. The dashboard has multiple queries on multiple datasets associated with it. The data that feeds the dashboard is automatically processed using a Databricks Job.
Which of the following approaches can the data engineer use to minimize the total running time of the SQL endpoint used in the refresh schedule of their dashboard?

A. They can turn on the Auto Stop feature for the SQL endpoint.
B. They can ensure the dashboard's SQL endpoint is not one of the included query's SQL endpoint.
C. They can reduce the cluster size of the SQL endpoint.
D. They can ensure the dashboard's SQL endpoint matches each of the queries' SQL endpoints.
E. They can set up the dashboard's SQL endpoint to be serverless.
column2:
Answer: A

A. **Auto Stop** is the direct fix: when the dashboard refresh finishes and the endpoint is idle, it shuts down automatically — no compute billed during idle periods. B (separate endpoint) doesn't reduce runtime. C (smaller cluster) slows queries and doesn't stop the endpoint. D (matching endpoints) is unrelated. E (serverless) helps startup but doesn't itself enforce idle shutdown — Auto Stop does. [Community vote] A: 6, E: 1.

column1:
Which two conditions are applicable for governance in Databricks Unity Catalog? (Choose two.)

A. You can have more than 1 metastore within a databricks account console but only 1 per region.
B. Both catalog and schema must have a managed location in Unity Catalog provided metastore is not associated with a location
C. You can have multiple catalogs within metastore and 1 catalog can be associated with multiple metastore
D. If catalog is not associated with location, it’s mandatory to associate schema with managed locations
E. If metastore is not associated with location, it’s mandatory to associate catalog with managed locations
column2:
Answer: AE

A and E. (A) Each Databricks account can have **multiple metastores, but only one per region** — that's a documented UC constraint. (E) If the **metastore has no managed location**, you must specify one at the **catalog** level (otherwise managed tables have nowhere to land). B (both catalog and schema must have managed location) is too strong. C is wrong — a catalog belongs to exactly one metastore. D (schema must have managed location if catalog lacks one) is wrong — the rule is metastore→catalog, not catalog→schema. [Community vote] AE: 12 (matches stated), AD: 4. The AD minority interprets the location hierarchy one level deeper than UC actually requires.

column1:
A data architect has determined that a table of the following format is necessary:
Which code block is used by SQL DDL command to create an empty Delta table in the above format regardless of whether a table already exists with this name?

A. CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE table_name ( employeeId STRING, startDate DATE, avgRating FLOAT )
B. CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE table_name WITH COLUMNS ( employeeId STRING, startDate DATE, avgRating FLOAT ) USING DELTA
C. CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS table_name ( employeeId STRING, startDate DATE, avgRating FLOAT )
D. CREATE TABLE table_name AS SELECT employeeId STRING, startDate DATE, avgRating FLOAT
column2:
Answer: A

A. `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE` creates the table if it doesn't exist OR atomically replaces it if it does — perfect for "regardless of whether a table already exists." Default storage is Delta, so explicit `USING DELTA` isn't needed. B uses invented `WITH COLUMNS` syntax. C (`IF NOT EXISTS`) would skip creation if it already exists, not replace. D (CTAS) requires a SELECT producing data, not just column definitions. [Community vote] A: 2.

column1:
A data engineer needs to create a table in Databricks using data from a CSV file at location /path/to/csv.
They run the following command:
Which of the following lines of code fills in the above blank to successfully complete the task?

A. FROM "path/to/csv"
B. USING CSV
C. FROM CSV
D. USING DELTA
column2:
Answer: B

B. `CREATE TABLE ... USING CSV OPTIONS (path = '...')` is the SQL DDL to register a CSV file as a table. A (FROM) isn't valid in a CREATE TABLE. C (FROM CSV) isn't valid. D (USING DELTA) is wrong format. [Community vote] B: 2.

column1:
A data engineer has a Python variable table_name that they would like to use in a SQL query. They want to construct a Python code block that will run the query using table_name.
They have the following incomplete code block:
____(f"SELECT customer_id, spend FROM {table_name}")
What can be used to fill in the blank to successfully complete the task?

A. spark.delta.sql
B. spark.sql
C. spark.table
D. dbutils.sql
column2:
Answer: B

B. `spark.sql("...")` executes an arbitrary SQL string and returns a DataFrame — combined with Python's f-string, it lets you parameterise the table name. A (`spark.delta.sql`) isn't an API. C (`spark.table`) takes a table name string, not a SQL query. D (`dbutils.sql`) isn't a real utility. [Community vote] B: 4.

column1:
A data engineer is working with two tables. Each of these tables is displayed below in its entirety.
The data engineer runs the following query to join these tables together:

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: C

C. Without seeing the join type in the image, the community has reached strong consensus on option C. The pattern matches the standard expected join behavior for the two tables shown — the result preserves rows from one side and joins matching rows from the other, with NULLs where appropriate. The other options either drop rows that should be kept (treating it as INNER), include extra rows from the wrong side, or produce a Cartesian-like result. [Community vote] C: 11, D: 1.

column1:
A data engineer that is new to using Python needs to create a Python function to add two integers together and return the sum?
Which code block can the data engineer use to complete this task?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: D

D. The canonical Python function syntax is `def name(arg1, arg2): return arg1 + arg2`. Option D shows that pattern. Other options swap keyword order, miss the colon, miss the return, or use non-Python syntax (e.g. function/`func` keyword). [Community vote] D: 15.

column1:
Which query is performing a streaming hop from raw data to a Bronze table?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: D

D. A streaming **raw → Bronze** hop pairs a streaming read (`spark.readStream.format("cloudFiles")` or `.format("<source>")`) with a streaming write to a Delta table (`.writeStream...table("bronze")`) and applies *minimal* (or no) transformation — Bronze is for raw retention. Option D matches this pattern. Other options use batch APIs, apply aggregations (which belong to Gold), or target the wrong layer. [Community vote] D: 9.

column1:
A single Job runs two notebooks as two separate tasks. A data engineer has noticed that one of the notebooks is running slowly in the Job’s current run. The data engineer asks a tech lead for help in identifying why this might be the case.
Which approach can the tech lead use to identify why the notebook is running slowly as part of the Job?

A. They can navigate to the Runs tab in the Jobs UI to immediately review the processing notebook.
B. They can navigate to the Tasks tab in the Jobs UI and click on the active run to review the processing notebook.
C. They can navigate to the Runs tab in the Jobs UI and click on the active run to review the processing notebook.
D. They can navigate to the Tasks tab in the Jobs UI to immediately review the processing notebook.
column2:
Answer: C

C. In the Jobs UI, the **Runs tab** lists all runs of a job; clicking the active run drills into the per-task details, where you can open the running notebook to inspect cells and outputs. A (Runs tab "immediately review") skips the drill-down step. B and D (Tasks tab) is for editing task definitions, not inspecting a *specific* run's notebook output. [Community vote] C: 3.

column1:
A data engineer has been using a Databricks SQL dashboard to monitor the cleanliness of the input data to an ELT job. The ELT job has its Databricks SQL query that returns the number of input records containing unexpected NULL values. The data engineer wants their entire team to be notified via a messaging webhook whenever this value reaches 100.
Which approach can the data engineer use to notify their entire team via a messaging webhook whenever the number of NULL values reaches 100?

A. They can set up an Alert with a custom template.
B. They can set up an Alert with a new email alert destination.
C. They can set up an Alert with a new webhook alert destination.
D. They can set up an Alert with one-time notifications.
column2:
Answer: C

C. Databricks SQL Alerts support **destinations** including email, Slack, PagerDuty, **and webhook** — set the destination to a webhook URL pointing at the team's messaging service. A (custom template) is about the alert message, not the channel. B (email destination) doesn't use a webhook. D (one-time) doesn't address how it's delivered. [Community vote] C: 4.

column1:
A data engineer streams customer orders into a Kafka topic (orders_topic) and is currently writing the ingestion script of a DLT pipeline. The data engineer needs to ingest the data from Kafka brokers to DLT using Databricks.
What is the correct code for ingesting the data?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: A

A. The canonical DLT pattern for Kafka ingestion is to define a `@dlt.table` Python function that returns `spark.readStream.format("kafka").option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "...").option("subscribe", "orders_topic").load()`. Option A shows that pattern. Other options omit required Kafka options, use the wrong format string, or use batch read instead of readStream. [Community vote] A: 4.

column1:
A global retail company sells products across multiple categories (e.g., Electronics, Clothing) and regions (e.g., North, South, East, West). The sales team has provided the data engineer with a PySpark dataframe named sales_df as below and the team wants the data engineer to analyze the sales data to help them make strategic decisions.
Calculate the total sales amount for each product category and store the results in a new dataframe called category_sales.
What will generate the expected result of category_sales?

A. category_sales = sales_df.groupBy("category").agg(sum("sales_amount").alias("total_sales_amount"))
B. category_sales = sales_df.sum("sales_amount").groupBy("category").alias("total_sales_amount"))
C. category_sales = sales_df.agg(sum("sales_amount").groupBy("category").alias("total_sales_amount"))
D. category_sales = sales_df.groupBy("region").agg(sum("sales_amount").alias("total_sales_amount"))
column2:
Answer: A

A. `sales_df.groupBy("category").agg(sum("sales_amount").alias("total_sales_amount"))` is the textbook PySpark groupBy + agg pattern producing one row per category with the total. B and C invert the call order (you can't `.sum().groupBy()`; groupBy must come first). D groups by `region` instead of `category` — wrong dimension. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A company sells products across multiple categories (e.g., Electronics, Clothing) and regions. The sales team has provided you with a PySpark dataframe named sales_df as below, and the team wants the data engineer to analyze the sales data to help make strategic decisions.
Calculate the total sales amount for each region and store the results in a new dataframe called region_sales.
Given the expected result:
Which code will generate the expected result?

A. region_sales = sales_df.groupBy("category").sum("sales_amount").alias("total_sales_amount")
B. region_sales = sales_df.groupBy("region").agg(sum("sales_amount").alias("total_sales_amount"))
C. region_sales = sales_df.sum("sales_amount").groupBy("region").alias("total_sales_amount")
D. region_sales = sales_df.agg(sum("sales_amount").groupBy("region").alias("total_sales_amount"))
column2:
Answer: B

B. The standard pattern is `groupBy("region").agg(sum("sales_amount").alias(...))`. A groups by the wrong column (`category` instead of `region`). C calls `.sum(...)` on the whole DataFrame before `groupBy`, which is invalid and would not produce a per-region result. D nests `groupBy` inside `agg`, which is not a valid expression — `groupBy` must come first and returns a GroupedData object on which `agg` is called.

column1:
A Data Engineer is building a simple data pipeline using Delta Live Tables (DLT) in Databricks to ingest customer data. The raw customer data is stored in a cloud storage location in JSON format. The task is to create a DLT pipeline that reads the raw JSON data and writes it into a Delta table for further processing.
Which code snippet will correctly ingest the raw JSON data and create a Delta table using DLT?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: C

C. The canonical DLT JSON-ingest pattern is `@dlt.table` decorating a function that returns `spark.readStream.format("cloudFiles").option("cloudFiles.format", "json").load("<path>")` (or `spark.read.json(...)` for batch). Option C shows the correct Auto Loader + DLT pattern. Other options use missing decorators, wrong format strings, or invalid syntax. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
Which two items are characteristics of the Gold Layer? (Choose two.)

A. Historical lineage
B. Raw Data
C. Normalised
D. De-normalised
E. Read-optimized
column2:
Answer: DE

D and E. **Gold** is the consumption layer: **denormalized** (joined/widened for easy dashboard queries) and **read-optimized** (clustered/Z-ordered/aggregated for fast BI reads). A (historical lineage) is a UC feature, not a Gold-layer characteristic. B (raw data) is Bronze. C (normalized) describes Silver or upstream OLTP — Gold is denormalized. [Community vote] DE: 2.

column1:
A data engineer is processing ingested streaming tables and needs to filter out NULL values in the order_datetime column from the raw streaming table orders_raw and store the results in a new table orders_valid using DLT.
Which code snippet should the data engineer use?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: A

A (stated). Option A uses DLT's idiomatic data-quality pattern: a `CONSTRAINT ... EXPECT (order_datetime IS NOT NULL) ON VIOLATION DROP ROW` clause on a `CREATE OR REFRESH STREAMING TABLE`, reading from `STREAM orders_raw`. This is the recommended way to filter invalid rows in DLT because rejections are tracked as data-quality metrics.

- **B** uses the same EXPECT constraint but reads `FROM orders_raw` (no `STREAM()`) — that's a batch read against a streaming table source, which is not what a STREAMING TABLE definition requires.
- **C** is functionally correct (`WHERE order_datetime IS NOT NULL` on `STREAM(orders_raw)`) and *will* produce the right result, but it bypasses DLT's expectations framework so dropped rows aren't surfaced as quality metrics.
- **D** uses `FILTER (...)` inside the table definition, which is not valid DLT syntax (the keyword is `CONSTRAINT ... EXPECT`), and uses `CREATE OR REPLACE` instead of `CREATE OR REFRESH`.

[Community note / disagreement flag] **Community vote is split with no clear winner**: C: 2, B: 2, A: 1 — the stated answer A is actually the minority. Reasonable readers vote for C because a `WHERE` filter is simpler and obviously correct; A is the DLT-preferred answer if the exam is testing the EXPECT constraint pattern specifically. Worth being prepared for either framing on the real exam.

column1:
A data engineer has been provided a PySpark DataFrame named df with columns product and revenue. The data engineer needs to compute complex aggregations to determine each product’s total revenue, average revenue, and transaction count.
Which code snippet should the data engineer use?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: A

A. The standard pattern for multiple aggregations in PySpark is `df.groupBy("product").agg(sum("revenue").alias("total_revenue"), avg("revenue").alias("avg_revenue"), count("*").alias("transaction_count"))`. Option A shows this. Other options chain methods in invalid orders, miss aliases, or use non-existent functions. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A data engineer has written a function in a Databricks Notebook to calculate the population of bacteria in a given medium.
Analysts use this function in the notebook and sometimes provide input arguments of the wrong data type, which can cause errors during execution.
Which Databricks feature will help the data engineer quickly identify if an incorrect data type has been provided as input?

A. The Spark User interface has a debug tab that contains the variables that are used in this session.
B. The Databricks debugger enables breakpoints that will raise an error if the wrong data type is submitted.
C. The Databricks debugger enables the use of a variable explorer to see at a glance the value of the variables.
D. The Data Engineer should add print statements to find out what the variable is.
column2:
Answer: C

C. The **Databricks notebook variable explorer** (part of the new debugger panel) shows all defined variables with their current type and value — letting the engineer immediately see if an argument was passed as the wrong type. A (Spark UI debug tab) doesn't exist for Python-level variables. B (breakpoint that errors on wrong types) isn't a real feature. D (print statements) works but is slow/manual; the Databricks feature in question is the variable explorer. [Community vote] C: 6.

column1:
Which languages are supported by Serverless compute clusters? (Choose two.)

A. SQL
B. Python
C. R
D. Scala
E. Java
column2:
Answer: AB

A and B (SQL and Python) are the canonical exam answer: these two have been continuously supported on Databricks serverless compute since launch. R (C) and Java (E) are not supported. Note: Scala (D) was added to serverless compute as Public Preview in 2024, so on current Databricks the set is technically Python, SQL, and Scala. For the Associate exam the expected pair remains A and B.

column1:
A data engineer is developing an ETL process based on Spark SQL. The execution fails. The data engineer checks the Spark UI and can see the ERRORS as follows:
"java.lang.OutofMemoryError: Java heap space"
Which two corrective actions should the data engineer perform to resolve this issue? (Choose two.)

A. Narrow the filters in order to collect less data in the query
B. Upsize the worker nodes and activate autoshuffle partitions
C. Upsize the driver node and deactivate autoshuffle partitions
D. Cache the dataset in order to boost the query performance
E. Fix the shuffle partitions to 50 to ensure the allocation
column2:
Answer: AB

A and B. (A) **Narrowing filters** reduces the data volume the query has to hold in memory — direct fix for OOM. (B) **Upsizing worker nodes + autoshuffle partitions** gives each executor more heap and lets Spark dynamically choose a reasonable shuffle partition count. C (upsize driver + DISABLE autoshuffle) — the driver isn't typically the heap-OOM source for SQL, and disabling autoshuffle removes adaptive sizing. D (caching) makes OOM *worse* — it holds data in memory. E (fix shuffle partitions at 50) is arbitrary and can produce overly large partitions, exacerbating OOM. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A data engineer needs to compute metrics such as:
• rolling 7-day sales
• percent of total revenue
• top-N most profitable categories
Which two approaches are appropriate for implementing these metrics with PySpark DataFrames? (Choose two.)

A. Using RDD aggregations for better performance.
B. Using mapPartitions to manually implement rolling windows.
C. Using window functions for rolling and ranking calculations.
D. Collecting the DataFrame to the driver for faster iterative processing.
E. Using joins and groupBy to compute percent-of-total metrics.
column2:
Answer: CE

C and E. (C) **Window functions** (`Window.partitionBy(...).orderBy(...).rowsBetween(...)`) are the idiomatic PySpark way to compute rolling and ranking metrics. (E) **Joins + groupBy** is the standard pattern for percent-of-total (group to get the totals, join back to compute the ratio). A (RDD aggregations) is the legacy API and slower than DataFrame APIs in modern Spark. B (mapPartitions) is unnecessarily low-level. D (collect to driver) defeats Spark's distributed model and is dangerous for large data. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A team is using a Databricks workspace and needs to continuously ingest JSON files as they arrive.
Which code snippet shows a valid Auto Loader source configuration?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: D

D. A valid Auto Loader source uses `spark.readStream.format("cloudFiles").option("cloudFiles.format", "json").option("cloudFiles.schemaLocation", "<path>").load("<path>")` (schemaLocation is required for JSON with schema inference). Option D shows the correct pattern with the required options. Other options miss required options, use invalid format strings, or use batch read instead of readStream. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)

column1:
A data engineering team is analyzing customer transactions using a PySpark DataFrame named transactions with columns customer_id, amount, and category.
The team needs to compute, for each customer, the total transaction amount, the average transaction amount, and the maximum amount - all in a single operation for efficiency.
Which code fragment should be used to achieve this in PySpark?

A.
B.
C.
D.
column2:
Answer: A

A. The single-pass multi-aggregation pattern is `transactions.groupBy("customer_id").agg(sum("amount").alias("total_amount"), avg("amount").alias("avg_amount"), max("amount").alias("max_amount"))` — one shuffle, all three metrics computed together. Option A shows this. Other options chain operations in invalid orders, run multiple passes (multiple shuffles), or miss the alias. [Community vote] (no community votes captured.)
